figures.caption: Figure 1 | Overview of SensorGen. We present a large-scale study of real-world sensor time-series generation, spanning 14 settings across 4 domains, 7 datasets, and 12 signal modalities. SensorGen establishes to date the broadest coverage of sequence length, frequency, and time span. More details are in Appendix B.1.
Table 1 | Comparison of studies on generative modeling of sensor time series.
Figure 3 | Task coverage of SensorGen.
Figure 4 | Illustration of task categories in SensorGen. For each category, we show the task-level data flow (left) and representative generation settings with example channels (right).
Figure 6 | Comparison of long-sequence generation. Models struggle with long signals in semanticto-signal generation, but outperform on imputation and translation when sensor context is available.

Figure 7 | Downstream utility of generated signals. With ECGs synthesized by a text-to-ECG generator, we evaluate their utility in two downstream settings. (a) Moderate synthetic augmentation improves disease classification performance, whereas excessive synthetic data degrades it. (b) Adding synthetic data improves zero-shot transfer in ECG-text representation learning.
Table 8 | Overview of datasets used in SensorGen.
Table 9 | Data configurations used in SensorGen. The segmented windows in each generation setting.
Table 10 | Performance comparison on Translation. SiT performs better on PPG-to-ECG translation while FractalGen is strong on Peripheral-to-EEG translation.
Table 11 | Forecasting and intervention-conditioned forecasting results. SiT performs best on medication-aware forecasting and remains competitive with MAR on PPG-DaLIA forecasting.
Table 13 | Performance comparison on Editing. Imagen performs better on CGM super-resolution.
Table 14 | Performance comparison on Translation. SiT performs better on all these settings.

Table 15 | Performance comparison on Semantic-to-Signal. SiT achieves overall better performance on activity-to-IMU and emotion-to-EEG generation, and Imagen achieves better performance on text-to-ECG task setting.
Table 16 | Performance comparison on training-free Editing. SiT achieves the best performance on ECG editing and ECG denoising, and DiT is the strongest on IMU editing task.

Table 17 | Downstream utility of generated signals. With ECGs synthesized by a text-to-ECG generator, we evaluate their utility supervised learning, and found moderate synthetic augmentation improves disease classification performance, whereas excessive synthetic data degrades it.

Table 18 | Downstream utility of generated signals. With ECGs synthesized by a text-to-ECG generator, we evaluate their utility in self-supervised learning. We found that adding synthetic data improves zero-shot transferability in ECG-text representation learning.
Table 19 | Comparison on normalization strategies of input signals. We report results on invasive blood pressure reconstruction. Range based normalization provides the best performance.
Table 20 | Comparison of long-sequence generation. Models perform better on imputation and translation tasks when signal-side context is available.
Table 21 | Comparison of long-sequence generation. Models struggle with long signals in semanticto-signal generation.
Table 22 | Comparisons of different levels of metrics. Feature space metrics are more related to signal fidelity. Here we report the cardiac-signal forecasting results.

Table 23 | Model capacity and training configuration in SensorGen. We instantiate each representative model with a comparable parameter scale whenever possible, while preserving its native architectural design and optimization convention.
Table 24 | Configurations of generation target and input conditions.
